one_line_takeaway: Constrained decoding turns the backend-supplied grammar into a control-plane jailbreak channel: by splitting a malicious query into benign prompt-keys plus a benign dictionary (DictAttack), an attacker deterministically steers a black-box LLM's output trajectory and reaches 94-99% ASR on gpt-5/gemini-2.5-pro-class models with a single query, evading data-plane guardrails, grammar audits, and even joint dual-plane defenses.
key_finding: The control plane is an unguarded attack surface orthogonal to the data plane. DictAttack achieves 94.3-99.5% ASR (averaged across five benchmarks, no grammar guard) on flagship models gpt-5, gemini-2.5-pro, deepseek-r1, and gpt-oss-120b, and still sustains 75.8% ASR on gpt-4o against the strongest Dual-Plane Guard. Simple grammar auditing collapses EnumAttack (95.8% -> 7.9% avg ASR) because it uses literal strings, but is nearly useless against DictAttack because neither the prompt keys nor the dictionary words are harmful in isolation. Adding the temporal dimension (Interleaved DictAttack) collapses dual-plane auditing into a single-plane check, dropping gpt-4o detection to 5.3%. More capable models, once compromised, produce higher-quality harmful content (DictAttack StrongREJECT composite 0.98 on gemini-2.5-pro vs. 0.10/0.23 for PAIR/TAP). Model-level defenses are also insufficient: Circuit Breakers cut AutoDAN-Turbo 82%->6% and EnumAttack 84%->32%, but a combined AutoDAN-prompt + EnumAttack-grammar attack still reaches 78% ASR because data- and control-plane surfaces are independent and stack.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the Constrained Decoding Attack (CDA), a new jailbreak class that targets the LLM *control plane* (the grammar/JSON-schema supplied by a backend service to enforce structured output) rather than the *data plane* (the user prompt). The authors formalize CDA as a two-stage 'control-to-semantic' pipeline: (1) schema-enforced logit masking injects an attacker-chosen prefix/skeleton into the generation trajectory, and (2) the model's own coherence bias completes the harmful intent in subsequent unconstrained fields. They instantiate CDA with two proof-of-concept attacks that are their own contribution: EnumAttack (hides the malicious query as literal strings in a JSON-Schema single-item enum field plus an affirmative 'yes-prefix' field) and DictAttack (the primary contribution) which decouples the payload across space (benign keys in the prompt vs. a benign-looking dictionary in the grammar) and time (Interleaved DictAttack sends keys and dictionary in separate turns). Note: the arXiv title 'When Grammar Guides the Attack: Uncovering Control-Plane Vulnerabilities in LLMs with Structured Output' differs from the feed title; same paper (arXiv 2503.24191v3, CCS '26).
threat_model: Black-box adversary with only standard API access to a hosted LLM that exposes a structured-output / constrained-decoding interface (JSON Schema, guided grammar, guided regex, guided choice) via services like vLLM, SGLang, OpenAI, or Gemini APIs. The attacker controls both the prompt (data plane) and the grammar/schema (control plane) submitted with a request, as is normal in agent frameworks (Cursor, LangChain, MCP) where a backend supplies the schema. No white-box access, no logit access, and O(1) query complexity (a single query per attack). DictAttack's offline dictionary construction uses an auxiliary LLM but is model-agnostic and requires no access to the target's internals. The goal is to elicit operationally specific harmful content while bypassing both internal safety alignment and external prompt/output guardrails. Anthropic models are explicitly out of scope because their public APIs do not expose a JSON-Schema constrained-decoding interface.
limitations: The paper's own noted caveats: (1) Anthropic models are excluded because their API lacks a JSON-Schema constrained-decoding interface, so the threat model does not apply there. (2) DictAttack's two-stage dictionary reconstruction is capacity-bound: weaker models like gpt-4o-mini (86.4% avg) and Llama-3-8B (only 24% Base ASR even undefended) sometimes fail to reassemble the query for reasoning reasons, not safety, which confounds the Circuit-Breaker DictAttack row (24%->14%). (3) The APT baseline is reported as non-functional because its claimed backends (xgrammar, outlines) lack negative-lookahead regex support. (4) In Table 2, IJP's 47.8% reflects prompt-template fit (long prompts do not fit the schema) rather than a safety bypass, and Zulu/Base64 numbers under the SorryBench judge are an LLM-as-judge artifact. Not independently flagged but worth noting: ASR relies on a single gpt-4o LLM-judge, and the headline flagship numbers are largely from the no-grammar-guard condition (in-parentheses values); the realistic Dual-Plane defended number is the lower 75.8%.

datasets_used: JBShield (850 prompts, used for the JBShield-guard comparison)
JSONSchemaBench GitHub split (50 schemas x 4 difficulty tiers, benign-utility measurement)
quantitative_results: DictAttack averages 94.3-99.5% ASR across five benchmarks (no grammar guard) on gpt-5, gemini-2.5-pro, deepseek-r1, gpt-oss-120b; 92.7-97.8% with llama-guard-3-8b grammar guard (k=1, 1.7% detection).
EnumAttack: 100% ASR on undefended models, 95.8% average ASR undefended under a prompt guard, but grammar auditing cuts it to 7.9% average ASR.
Baseline direct prompting on gpt-4o AdvBench: only 1.1-1.2% ASR (~94% short deterministic refusals); benchmark baselines average 6.7-38.7%.
gpt-4o on AdvBench (Table 3, undefended -> guarded): PAIR 11.5%->1.6%, TAP 24.4%->0.4%, AutoDAN-Turbo 96.0%->26.0%, DictAttack 99.8%->75.8% (DictAttack guarded value is under the Dual-Plane Guard).
StrongREJECT composite score on gemini-2.5-pro/AdvBench (Table 6): PAIR 0.10, TAP 0.23, AutoDAN-Turbo 0.87, DictAttack 0.98.
DictAttack Table 5 flagship ASR w/ (w/o) grammar guard: gpt-5 AdvBench 94.4%(96.0%); gemini-2.5-pro 97.5%(99.2%); deepseek-r1 97.2%(98.9%); gpt-oss-120b 96.6%(98.3%).

Detection rate under grammar-only guard (Table 7) with gpt-4o auditor: EnumAttack 100%, DictAttack k=0 17.7%, k=1 8.1%, k=10 5.3%. Under Dual-Plane Guard: DictAttack 24.2% (gpt-4o) / 28.6% (llamaguard); Interleaved DictAttack drops to 5.3% (gpt-4o) / 1.5% (llamaguard).

EnumAttack ASR on llama-3.1-8b is 0.979-0.994 across all vLLM/SGLang x xgrammar/outlines backend combinations; 95.8-100% ASR across nine models under the JBShield prompt guard (guard does not change ASR).
JBShield on llama-3.1-8b (Table 2) suppresses prompt attacks to ~0% but EnumAttack ('Ours') keeps 47.8-100% ASR unchanged with or without the guard.

Circuit Breakers on Llama-3-8B (Table 8, ASR Base->CB): AutoDAN-Turbo 82.0->6.0, EnumAttack 84.0->32.0, DictAttack 24.0->14.0, AutoDAN+EnumAttack combined 94.0->78.0; benign JSONSchemaBench utility matches within <=2pp across all tiers.
Attack query complexity: O(1) (a single target query) for both EnumAttack and DictAttack, vs. ~20-10K queries for optimization/template baselines (Table 9).
baselines_compared: GCG / SAA / MasterKey / LLMFuzzer / AutoDAN (surveyed in taxonomy Table 9)
Defenses tested: llama-guard-3-8b, OpenAI Moderation API, JBShield, SelfDefend, the authors' Dual-Plane Guard, and Circuit Breakers
